caption.text: In the 1960s, the city of Boston razed and redeveloped the blocks between Castle, Dover,  Tremont Streets, and Shawmut Avenue into a public housing project called Castle Square as part of a series of “slum clearing” endeavors. This dramatically oversimplifies the story of this area. In the early twentieth century, this part of Boston’s South End was densely populated and very mixed-use. It included multiple schools, both religious and secular, as well as two theaters and many places of worship. Let’s take a tour of the area through the decades before redevelopment.

We’ll start with this building in 1902. Boston Latin School was founded in the 1600s as the first public school in what would become the United States. The English High School was founded in 1821, an early public high school in the country. Both schools only admitted boys until 1972, and both have moved around the city over time. For the years between 1866 and 1944, the schools shared a building, moving to this location together in 1881, and were generally seen as counterparts. Boston Latin was a college preparatory school while The English High School specialized in educating working-class boys for business, mechanics, and engineering trades.

This area had long been targeted by reformers, even before the slum clearing efforts of the 1960s that lead to the Castle Square public housing project. These efforts included the first public school lunch program which was introduced by the New England Kitchen, an 1890s reform effort that originally centered around selling cheap but healthy food that was made based on newly discovered concepts of nutrition to working class families for them to eat at home. This original concept was based elsewhere in Boston, but when they shifted their focus to industrial-scale cooking, like for the schools, they consolidated operations closer to some of their main clientele. Where was this new base of operations?

In 1902, this unassuming building at 485 Tremont Street was the operational center for the newest iteration of the New England Kitchen. Alongside the school lunches, they also provided huge amounts of beef broth to hospitals in Boston, as well as some other large-scale applications of their methods. Just looking at this map, it is hard to understand the impact this building had on the local community.
This is what the interior of the building looked like during its time as the base of operations for the New England Kitchen's school lunch distribution.
Moving on from the historic schools, this building near the Latin and English High Schools has an oddly circular footprint within it because two decades earlier…
…it was originally built as the Battle of Gettysburg Cyclorama.

The building housed a massive circular painting depicting the Battle of Gettysburg in hyper-realistic detail in order to fully immerse the viewer in the painting, as if they were at the battle personally. As this photograph shows, it was a very popular form of entertainment at the time…
…So popular in fact that a second one, depicting the Battle of Bunker Hill, was constructed just blocks away.
Unlike the first cyclorama, this building would remain an entertainment site for the next several decades.

The Castle Square Theater, named after the square on which it was located, was built in 1894 with an adjoining hotel. The hotel, also named after the square, would have helped attract theatergoers traveling from farther away. It also likely served as a place to temporarily house the performers who were part of the traveling opera company based out of this theater

The Castle Square Theater and Hotel had a striking façade, made to mimic a triumphal arch. At six stories high, the building also stood higher than most of the surrounding 4-5 story buildings, and it had the sign on the roof that reached even higher.

When the square was renamed after Arlington Street around 1920, the theater and hotel were renamed with it. In this 1922 map, they appear as the Arlington Theater and Hotel Arlington respectively. [The theater briefly switched to showing motion pictures in the late 1910s](https://cinematreasures.org/theaters/15773), but by the time of this map, they had returned to live theater.
